name: master pesto pasta
instructions:
TWO VARIANTS BY TECHNIQUE — PESTLE OR BLENDER. The choice of tool determines which ingredients work; this is not just a matter of how fine the chop is.

VARIANT A — PESTLE STYLE:
- Soft greens only (basil canonical; spinach and baby arugula / mixed greens also work).
- Soft nuts only (pine nuts canonical; cashews and sunflower seeds also fine).
- Pound greens, nuts, garlic, olive oil, parmesan, black pepper into a rough paste.
- Toss with hot pasta. Optional finish: a splash of heavy whipping cream for richness, or fresh chopped tomatoes for brightness.

VARIANT B — BLENDER STYLE:
- Inclusive greens (basil + any of: parsley, kale, asparagus, carrot greens, spinach, mixed greens).
- All nuts work (pine, almonds, cashews, sunflower seeds).
- Liquid component is load-bearing: heavy whipping cream (canonical) or lemon juice. Don't try to blend on olive oil alone.
- Canned tomatoes are structural here — blend smooth, the concentrated flavor integrates with the green pesto base.
- Optional: sauté yellow onion briefly before blending to soften the raw bite.
- Crunchy add at the end: slivered almonds or sunflower seeds folded in AFTER blending for textural contrast (toasted is better).
- Toss with PENNE or other tubular/short pasta — NOT spaghetti.
notes:
TWO TECHNIQUES, TWO INGREDIENT LISTS — THE TOOL DETERMINES THE RECIPE:

PESTLE RULES — SOFT THINGS ONLY:
- GREENS: basil, baby arugula (mixed greens), spinach. Parsley does NOT work in a pestle — it's too fibrous to break down by hand.
- NUTS: pine nuts, cashews, sunflower seeds. ALMONDS do NOT work in a pestle — too hard, you can't break them down without a blender or food processor.
- TOMATOES: fresh only, finely chopped and rinsed, as a secondary addition. Canned tomatoes are wrong texture for a mortar dish.
- GARLIC HANDLING: garlic in pestle pesto is potent. Two ways to manage: (a) use sparingly — one clove per batch, or (b) pre-macerate in lemon juice for a few minutes, then drain aggressively before pestling.
- CREAM OPTIONAL: a splash of heavy whipping cream stirred in at the end works as a richer finish. Not traditional but fine.
- RAISINS OPTIONAL: a small handful is a sicilian-leaning sweet-savory move that works with pine-nut + basil.

BLENDER RULES — INCLUSIVE:
- GREENS: basil works, but so do parsley, kale, asparagus, carrot greens. Spinach and mixed greens fine. Mix and match — the blender breaks down fibrous things the pestle can't.
- NUTS: all of pine nuts, almonds, cashews, sunflower seeds.
- VEGETABLES AND FIBER ARE WELCOME: blender pesto loses fiber vs pestle, so adding more vegetables (asparagus, kale, carrot greens, yellow onion) restores nutritional density and body.
- HEAVY CREAM IS LOAD-BEARING (or LEMON JUICE): the blender needs a liquid component to blend smooth. Heavy cream is canonical; lemon juice can stand in for a lighter version. Don't rely on olive oil alone — the result reads greasy rather than rich.
- CANNED TOMATOES SHINE HERE: the concentrated flavor integrates with the green pesto base after blending. Primary ingredient, not a substitute.
- CRUNCH AT THE END: slivered almonds and sunflower seeds folded in AFTER blending — restores some of the chew you lose by blending everything smooth. Toasted better than raw.
- ONION HANDLING: yellow onion in the blender works but raw bite can be harsh. Sauté briefly before blending.

STORE-BOUGHT BASIL PESTO IS CLASHING IN BOTH VARIANTS: jarred pesto isn't good enough for either technique. The pestle has texture and freshness a jar can't match; the blender has volume and inclusion logic a jar wasn't built for. Don't substitute.

BLENDER FAILURE MODES (learned the hard way):

- TOO MUCH PASTA WATER AT THE END: unlike the pestle version, the blender base already carries lots of liquid (cream, lemon, canned tomatoes). Adding pasta water — the standard pasta-finishing move — can instantly ruin the dish. Use a fraction of what you'd add to a regular pasta. Err on the dry side.

- SPAGHETTI INSTEAD OF PENNE: blender pesto is chunky, blended-and-rebuilt — closer to a pasta salad than traditional pasta-with-sauce. It needs a shape that holds chunks and picks up sauce in pockets. Penne (or other tubular/short shapes) is right. Spaghetti slides under the chunky sauce structure and reads wrong.

- TOO MUCH PROTEIN (NUT/SEED OVERLOAD): the blender makes it tempting to keep adding nuts to thicken the sauce. Past a certain point the dish swings PROTEIN SHAKE — gritty, dense, one-note. If the sauce is too thin, add more canned tomato or reduce — don't add more nuts.
